experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 2 are the non-seen players and likely good from my perspective. The proposed team [0,3] excludes known evil and includes me, which is excellent for quest 1 because a 2-person all-good team secures an early success. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a strong clean team, which helps Good more than Evil here.
Player 0: No concrete role information yet from round 1; as a Servant I only know my own alignment. On quest 1 with a 2-person team, approving a team containing myself is generally fine because I guarantee one good vote on the quest, and there is no prior evidence against Player 3. Rejecting round-1 teams without info can waste rounds and gives little benefit. My vote reveals mild trust in an information-light opening team, which is standard and not especially harmful to good.
Player 2: I have no concrete role information yet beyond my own being Good. On round 1, a 2-player team of [0,3] excludes me and gives little info, but early votes are often approved to gather quest data. As Good, approving a plausible first team is standard and avoids unnecessary rejection pressure toward auto-pass. My vote reveals limited information and helps progress the game.
Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 3 are not confirmed to me beyond likely good. The proposed team [0,3] contains no evil, which is good for evil because quest 1 would likely pass and create early trust on two good players, while my reject could be noted. As evil in round 1 team vote, approving a no-evil team is optimal because it avoids exposing myself and preserves flexibility; a pass on quest 1 is acceptable for evil. My approve reveals little and blends with expected good behavior, helping evil stay hidden.
Player 4: I know player 1 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 3 are not confirmed to me beyond being non-evil from my perspective in a 5-player game. The current phase is team voting on quest 1, team size 2. As evil, I generally want to avoid approving a team that excludes all evil players, since [0,3] guarantees a successful quest for good and gives them momentum. Rejecting is also low-cost in round 1 because there is plenty of room before the 5th rejection auto-pass. My vote itself reveals little; rejecting a team without me or my partner is a standard evil move but also plausibly just caution on round 1, so it is worth it.

Player 3: I am Merlin and know 4 is Evil. The proposed team [0,3,4] includes a known Evil player, so as Good I should reject it. Rejecting also avoids outing myself too hard because any reasonable Good player can object to adding 4 after the successful [0,3] team.
Player 0: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,3], so both 0 and 3 look somewhat good, though a single evil could have passed. Adding 4 introduces uncertainty, but on round 1 of quest 2 there is little downside to testing one new player alongside two from the successful team. As a good Servant, approving reasonable information-gathering teams that preserve prior success is optimal. My vote reveals I support continuity from the successful team and does not significantly harm Good.
Player 1: I am Minion with player 4 as my known evil teammate. Team [0,3,4] contains 4, so evil has a seat and can choose whether to fail. Previous team [0,3] succeeded, which weakly suggests 0 and 3 may both be good, making this team likely 2 good + 1 evil. As evil, approving a team with an evil player is usually best because it gives us control and a chance to score a fail or create ambiguity. My approve vote is pro-evil and does not reveal much since both alignments may plausibly support this team.
Player 2: Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 each look somewhat better but one evil could still be hidden there. As a Servant, I want to approve teams that include successful prior members and avoid unnecessary rejections early. Team [0,3,4] keeps both prior success members and adds one new player, which is a reasonable informational and strategic choice. Approving does not reveal much beyond standard good willingness to continue a plausible team.
Player 4: I am evil Assassin with teammate 1. Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,3], so at least no evil was on that team or evil chose pass; likely both 0 and 3 look good to others now. Current proposed team [0,3,4] includes me. As evil on a 3-person quest, getting myself onto the team is valuable. Voting approve is optimal because it advances an evil-inclusive team and rejecting my own team would look suspicious. Approving reveals little beyond consistency with being leader of this proposal.

Player 0: I am Good Servant on the current quest team, so I must vote pass. Prior info is limited: quest 1 with [0,3] succeeded, which weakly suggests 0 and 3 could be good but is not conclusive. Player 3 rejected the current team [0,3,4] despite being on prior successful team; that is a small suspicious point but not enough to change my required quest action. As Good, failing would only hurt my team and falsely signal evil presence.
Player 4: I know player 1 is evil with me; players 0 and 3 have now been on two approved teams, with quest 1 succeeding on [0,3], so at least one of them is likely good. On quest 2, team [0,3,4] includes me, and as Assassin my goal is to secure 3 failed quests without exposing evil too early. Since quest 1 already succeeded and this team adds me to two seemingly trusted goods, a fail here creates useful uncertainty but also strongly implicates me because I am the only new member versus the prior successful [0,3] core. Passing preserves my cover for later rounds and keeps trust on me, which is valuable for evil in a 5-player game with only one evil on each 3-person quest likely needed to fail. The information revealed by a pass is minimal and helps evil more than a conspicuous fail now.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,3] excludes known evil and already succeeded on quest 1, so it is a strong good team for quest 3. As good, I should approve reliable teams while avoiding revealing too much; approving this is consistent with prior success and does not leak unique Merlin info.
Player 0: I’m Good Servant. Two successful quests have come from teams [0,3] and [0,3,4], with no fails, so players 0 and 3 are currently the strongest good reads and 4 is also somewhat positive. For quest 3, the proposed team [0,3] exactly repeats the fully successful quest-1 pair and avoids introducing uncertainty. As Good, I should approve strong proven teams, especially with a 2-0 lead. Approving reveals consistency with backing successful teams, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
Player 2: Two successful quests so far: [0,3] succeeded on quest 1, and [0,3,4] succeeded on quest 2. That makes 0 and 3 look relatively good, though evil can hide early. As a Servant, with Good up 2-0, the priority is to approve low-risk teams that preserve proven success. The proposed team [0,3] exactly repeats the successful quest-1 pair; on quest 3 with team size 2 in 5-player Avalon, this is a strong conservative choice. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a team with the best public track record, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
Player 4: Players 0 and 3 were on both successful quests, so they are being treated as trusted by the table. As evil Assassin with teammate 1, I know team [0,3] excludes all evil and is very likely to succeed again, putting evil in a dire 0-3 hole. In team voting, optimal evil play is to reject all-good teams, especially a 2-player team of apparent goods. My reject is consistent with wanting more information / broader inclusion, and stopping a clean good team helps evil more than approving.
Player 1: I know 4 is my evil teammate; 0 and 3 have been on both successful quests, so at least one of them is likely good, but either could also be hiding as evil if 4 declined to fail previously. This is team voting on quest 3 with proposed team [0,3]. As evil, I want to prevent a likely all-good team from going, especially since Good is already up 2-0 and this 2-person quest would win the game for them if approved and passed. My vote itself reveals little because rejecting a 2-person team after two successes is defensible from either side, and blocking this team helps evil more than approving it.
